nativeRange.habitat: Inland river systems, swamps, streams, reservoirs, and farm dams at low to medium elevations across central and eastern Australia. Tolerates both turbid and clear water and has been reported to survive multi-year droughts by lying dormant in deep burrows.

decorPreferences: PVC pipe sections or terracotta pots as burrows
sand or fine gravel substrate suitable for digging
tight-fitting lid with all gaps sealed
strong filtration with crayfish-safe intake guard
leaf litter and alder cones for tannins and grazing

diet.notes: Generally considered an opportunistic omnivore that has been documented eating detritus, plant matter, algae, sinking pellets, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, peas, lettuce), and animal protein (frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mussel, prawn, small feeder fish). Calcium-rich foods and supplements (cuttlebone, snail shells, mineral blocks) are commonly recommended to support molting. Has been reported to prey on slow-moving tankmates and to graze most live plants, so live planted setups are generally considered impractical.

goodWith: fast mid- and top-dwelling fish too large to be caught (e.g., adult rainbowfish, larger barbs, danios)
robust species that stay clear of the substrate
avoidWith: other yabbies in tanks without abundant individual hides (cannibalism on molting individuals is commonly reported)
other crayfish species (territorial conflict and disease vectoring)
dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates (treated as prey)
slow or bottom-dwelling fish (corydoras, plecos, kuhli loaches, bettas)
live planted-tank inhabitants (plants are typically destroyed)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by OpenCage, 2735x2736. Sharp, well-lit aquarium shot of a whole adult yabby on gravel; olive-green/blue-grey body with characteristic orange claw bands clearly visible. Best display-quality image.
Photo by TarnishedPath, 1701x2268. Crisp full-body shot of a pale blue/white ornamental colour morph on rock; adds colour-morph variety to the wild-type pick.

Photo by TarnishedPath, 3024x2268 (landscape orientation, animal shown vertically). Top-down view showing vivid electric-blue leg and claw edging; minor chromatic fringing on the limbs. Whole animal in frame.

summary: A large, generally hardy Australian freshwater crayfish widely kept in the aquarium hobby, including a popular selectively-bred bright blue color morph. Generally considered aggressive and escape-prone, it is best housed alone in a long, securely-lidded tank.
careNotes: The Yabby (Cherax destructor) is the most widespread Australian native crayfish, occurring across Victoria, New South Wales, southern Queensland, South Australia, and parts of the Northern Territory. Wild coloration is highly variable and is generally reported to track water clarity — darker (black, dark brown) in clear water and lighter (green-brown, beige, light pink) in turbid water — while selectively-bred 'Electric Blue' and 'Blue Yabby' morphs dominate the ornamental trade and share identical husbandry requirements with the wild type. Like all freshwater crayfish, the Yabby is highly sensitive to copper-based medications; any treatment containing copper should be avoided in a tank where this species is kept. Temperature recommendations vary noticeably across sources, with figures ranging from as low as 10°C to as high as 28°C; a practical window for aquarium keeping falls between 15°C and 26°C, which aligns with typical home-aquarium room temperatures. Importantly, the species is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to habitat degradation, and is regulated or prohibited in several jurisdictions outside Australia — notably in parts of the European Union and in some US states — due to its potential as an invasive species. Keepers should verify local regulations before purchase.
breedingNotes: Generally considered easy to breed in captivity given stable parameters, adequate calcium, and abundant hides. Females reach sexual maturity around 1 year of age at roughly 6-10 cm body length and have been reported to spawn 2-3 times per year, carrying 300-450 eggs ('berried') under the abdomen for approximately 19-40 days depending on water temperature. Newly hatched juveniles cling briefly to the female before dispersing as miniature adults; survival is improved by abundant micro-hides (mesh, plant clumps, fine rubble) since cannibalism on small or molting individuals is commonly reported. Juveniles can typically co-habitate with the mother if sufficient cover is provided. Hybridisation with related Cherax species (e.g., C. albidus, C. quadricarinatus) has been documented and is generally discouraged for aquarium-trade integrity.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder
primary.notes: Designated primary (Practical Fishkeeping) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden; swapped to Aquarium Breeder as the most comprehensive substitute. Source for adult size 10-20 cm, lifespan 3-8 years, temperature optimum 25-28°C with broad tolerance, pH 7.5-8.5 (tolerates 7-9), gH 6-12, kH 4-6, 30-40 gallon (120-160 L) minimum, breeding 350-450 eggs, molting frequency, omnivore diet, very aggressive temperament, high escape risk.

additional.name: World of Aquariums
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed adult size ~20 cm (7.9 in), lifespan 3-5 years, 40-gal (~151 L) minimum, semi-aggressive rating 6/10, pH 7-8, temperature 10-24°C, GH 8-20, bright blue color variant. Substitute for designated secondary Fishkeeping World which has no Yabby page.

additional.name: Aquatic Solutions Australia
additional.notes: Australian retailer care sheet — source for native range (SA, VIC, NSW, QLD, NT), 'optimal' temperature 12-20°C, pH 7.0-8.0, GH 50-300 ppm, 'best escape artists' note, aggressive predator temperament, and blue claw / electric blue trade morphs.

additional.name: CO2Art — Blue Yabby in the Freshwater Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed Blue Yabby color morph husbandry: up to 15 cm adult, 4-5 year wild lifespan (longer in captivity), 20-28°C, pH 7.5+, 300-350 eggs 2-3x yearly, 3-week incubation, tight-lid requirement, supplemental feeding (meat + vegetables + alder cones).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Common Yabby
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback (Parastacidae, Decapoda); confirmed native distribution across central and eastern Australia, 10-20 cm average size (up to 30 cm), drought-survival burrowing behavior, color-vs-water-clarity variation, IUCN Vulnerable listing, and invasive status in Western Australia and Tasmania.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molting frequency reportedly varies dramatically with age and growth rate: hatchlings have been observed shedding every 4–7 days, juveniles every 3–4 weeks, and mature adults only 1–2 times per year. Younger animals molt far more frequently than adults. The discarded exoskeleton is typically re-consumed for calcium and should not be removed. Cannibalism on freshly-molted (soft-shell) individuals is commonly reported in shared housing — abundant individual hides are essential. Insufficient calcium or unstable water has been associated with incomplete-molt fatalities; cuttlebone, mineral blocks, or hard-water keeping is generally recommended.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Readily breeds in captivity in stable, well-aerated water with calcium supplementation and ample hides. See top-level breedingNotes for clutch size, incubation, and rearing details.
